Step 2: elimination of –OH

Cyclic Sugars at Equilibrium

Section 23.9
CARBOHYDRATE CONFORMATION: THE ANOMERIC EFFECT

Chair Conformations of Pyranoses
Pyranoses adopt conformations that are chair-like
We expect an equatorial group at the anomeric carbon to be more stable than an axial group:

Chair Conformations of Pyranoses-1
Pyranoses adopt conformations that are chair-like
We expect an equatorial group at the anomeric carbon to be more stable than an axial group:
This is not observed! What’s going on?
Something must be stabilizing the equatorial Cl
The Anomeric Effect

Other Applications
The anomeric effect is also relevant to conformations of acycylic ethers, sulfides, and other X–C–Y–R groups
An anti orientation of the lone pair and C–X is preferred

Section 23.10
KETOSES

Ketoses
Ketoses are important intermediates in the biosynthesis of carbohydrates
Examples of well studied C-2 ketoses:

Cyclic Forms of Ketoses
Ketoses are mostly cyclized, just like aldoses
The anomeric carbon bears –R instead of –H

Section 23.11
DEOXY SUGARS

Deoxy Sugars
In deoxy sugars, one of the –OH groups is replaced by –H
2-Deoxy- d -ribose is particularly important! (Deoxyribo …)

Section 23.12
AMINO SUGARS
Amino Sugars

Imino Sugars
Sugars in which nitrogen is involved in cyclization are called imino sugars

Section 23.13
BRANCHED-CHAIN CARBOHYDRATES

Branched-chain Carbohydrates
Branched-chain carbohydrates contain a carbon group attached to the main chain
How else can we classify vancosamine?

Section 23.14
GLYCOSIDES: THE FISCHER GLYCOSIDATION

Glycosides
Glycosides contain a group other than –OH at the anomeric position
Prefix indicates the heteroatom bound to the anomeric carbon (e.g., O -, N -, or S -glycosides)
O -glycosides are mixed acetals, derived from the acid-catalyzed reaction of a monosaccharide with an alcohol

Fischer Glycosidation
Fischer glycosidation: treatment of a monosaccharide with an acid catalyst and an alcohol
Thermodynamic control gives an O -glycoside with some stereoselectivity
Mechanism may be endocyclic or exocyclic

Endocyclic Glycosidation
Step 4: protonation of –OH

Endocyclic Glycosidation-1
Step 6: Cyclization of the oxonium ion.

Endocyclic Glycosidation-2
Step 7: deprotonation to give neutral product and regenerate catalyst
What might the exocyclic mechanism look like?

Reactions of Glycosides
O -glycosides are stable to base but undergo hydrolysis in the presence of aqueous acid
Mechanism is essentially the reverse of glycosidation
